opp.next_step: 1/05/26 RH - F/up email to Jace - did she secure go-ahead to include Procurement Transformation in 26' plans. Major issues with Coupa impacting business - 50% supplier adoption, poor user adoption (prefer natural language of Slack) very manual, tedious intervention on invoices.
opp.ais_why:
• Jace Pennisi called Slack-integrated conversational procurement intake a hard requirement and the gating criterion for whether Zycus is invited to the RFI.
• She also flagged Coupa CLM as having no AI today and invited Zycus to position its CLM as an alternative, opening an explicit AI-displacement window.
• Two recorded calls including a 104-minute Nov demo workshop plus an active RFI/RFP roadmap to 2027 give high-confidence Hungry read.

sources: Avoma discovery call 20 October 2025, Jace Pennisi: '120 hours per year of manual approvals', 'rigid approval thresholds', 'inflexible'
Avoma demo workshop 20 November 2025, Jace Pennisi: 'AP team member spends ~5 minutes reviewing each invoice', '4 buyers + 1 onboarding lead manages $1.5B spend', 'headcount capped next year'
Avoma 20 November 2025: 'AI bot answered 40% of Slack queries with <30 minutes response time, down from 100% manual'
metrics.narrative: Etsy articulated quantified operational pain on the 20 October 2025 discovery call and validated it in the 20 November 2025 demo workshop. Jace Pennisi stated Coupa's rigid approval thresholds force 120 manual approval hours per year on fully matched requisitions that should auto-clear, and AP spends approximately 5 minutes per invoice on manual dummy-record creation due to poor extraction (20 October and 20 November calls). The procurement ops team of 4 buyers plus 1 onboarding lead manages $1.5 billion in spend with headcount capped for next year, so automation ROI directly unlocks capacity (20 November). Jace also noted a Slack AI bot answered 40% of queries in under 30 minutes, down from 100% manual, proving appetite for conversational AI intake (20 November). Pain owner is Jace Pennisi, Manager Procurement Operations. The value case was never formalized into a written ROI model or savings projection, so while the operational burden is quantified the financial impact (cost per approval hour, AP FTE cost, capacity unlock value) remains unquantified. Converting these figures into a dollar-anchored business case tied to Etsy's Coupa contract expiry forcing function is the missing step.

champion.narrative: Jace Pennisi, Manager Procurement Operations, was the early champion. She drove the exploration, articulated the pain, validated the demo enthusiastically ('This is already way easier than what my team has to do today in Coupa. Oh my god.' on 20 November 2025), and committed to recommending Zycus for the RFI post-Thanksgiving 2025 budget approval. She has access to her Director (Guru) and stated she would present the business case to him. Her advocacy was active through November 2025: she scheduled the demo, invited a Dublin-based buyer (though it is unclear if that person attended), and defined the mandatory Slack integration requirement. However, her engagement has been silent for 137 days since 9 February 2026. The rep's 5 January 2026 follow-up asked if she secured budget approval; no reply is logged. It is unknown whether she (a) could not secure internal approval and the project was shelved, (b) was told to explore other vendors, (c) is buried in other priorities, or (d) has simply ghosted. Her current status is at-risk: she showed strong early advocacy but has gone completely dark for nearly five months with no explanation. The relationship is fragile and requires immediate re-engagement to understand what changed between November 2025 (enthusiastic validation) and today (total silence). Until she resurfaces and re-confirms intent, treating her as an active champion is optimistic; she is a cooling champion with unexplained disengagement.

sources: Avoma 20 October 2025, Jace Pennisi: 'Coupa feels aging and not built for a nimble company like Etsy', 'we're not totally convinced it's gonna be Coupa for another 3 years'
Avoma 20 October 2025: 'Coupa: dated UI, inflexible approval thresholds, poor customer support, weak AI capabilities'
Avoma 20 October 2025: 'formal RFI is planned by January to shortlist P2P solutions' (3–5 vendors typical)
Avoma 20 November 2025: Zycus demo validated Slack integration, dynamic workflows, intelligent AP, single-platform CLM
SF Next_Step 5 January 2026: 'Major issues with Coupa impacting business - 50% supplier adoption, poor user adoption'
competition.narrative: The primary competitor is the incumbent Coupa, and the competitive dynamic is displacement, not a head-to-head bake-off. Jace Pennisi stated on 20 October 2025 that Etsy is 'not totally convinced it's gonna be Coupa for another 3 years' and described multiple weaknesses: aging platform not suited for software-centric procurement, inflexible workflows, poor customer support ('no face-to-face interaction… they just give us community links'), and weak AI capabilities. She contrasted this with their positive Certa partnership. The Coupa contract expires in 2027, creating the forcing function. Jace's stated plan was to run an RFI in January 2026 to 'find a handful, maybe slightly more than a handful of people that could maybe do what we're asking,' shortlist via RFI, then issue an RFP and transition by contract end. The question is whether Etsy issued that RFI to other vendors and Zycus was excluded, or whether the entire procurement transformation was paused. No other P2P vendor (Ivalua, SAP Ariba, Jaggaer, GEP) was named on any call, but the RFI process would have brought in 3–5 alternatives. The 137-day silence suggests either (a) Etsy moved forward with other vendors and Zycus is out, (b) the initiative was shelved due to budget/priority changes, or (c) Jace could not secure approval and the project died. Our wedge was validated on 20 November 2025: Slack-native conversational intake (mandatory requirement, Zycus roadmap confirmed for end of year 2025), dynamic approval bypass (120 hours/year pain), intelligent invoice extraction (5 min/invoice pain), single-platform CLM with no data silos (vs Coupa's acquired/bolted modules), and proactive partnership (the Certa contrast). The win strategy is to (1) re-engage Jace and confirm project status, (2) if alive, prove Slack integration is live and schedule a follow-up demo, (3) expand to her Director and strategic finance to build multi-thread sponsorship, (4) anchor the business case to the Coupa expiry forcing function with a quantified ROI model. If the RFI already went out and Zycus was excluded, this is a loss; if the project is paused, the move is to keep Zycus top-of-mind for when it restarts; if it is alive and Jace is simply overwhelmed, urgency and executive engagement can revive it.

Avoma 20 October 2025: 'Lacks human, face-to-face support; relies on community links instead of proactive help'

SF Next_Step 5 January 2026: '50% supplier adoption, poor user adoption (prefer natural language of Slack) very manual, tedious intervention on invoices'
Avoma 20 November 2025: 'Sourcing events over $250K run outside of Coupa via spreadsheets', 'Contract redlining happens in Word; Coupa CLM averages 14 days per contract and lacks user training'
identify_pain.narrative: Etsy's pain with Coupa is pervasive, clearly articulated, and multi-dimensional. On 20 October 2025 Jace Pennisi stated 'Coupa feels aging and not built for a nimble company like Etsy… very rarely buy physical things, and there's just a lot of limitations in terms of how to build processes.' She described poor vendor partnership: 'We don't find that level of partnership with Coupa… there's just not that level of human communication… no face-to-face interaction.' She contrasted this with their Certa (supplier onboarding) vendor: 'They're fabulous… the first to reach out… willing to discuss… partnership has grown significantly.' Operational pain quantified: 120 manual approval hours per year due to inflexible thresholds (20 October and 20 November), 5 minutes per invoice for AP manual dummy-record intervention (20 November), and poor user adoption ('50% supplier adoption, poor user adoption, prefer natural language of Slack' per 5 January Next_Step). The sourcing team works outside Coupa in spreadsheets for events over $250K, and contract redlining happens in Word because Coupa CLM is cumbersome and untrained (20 November). Pain owner is Jace Pennisi, Manager Procurement Operations, who is accountable for the team's efficiency and the user experience. The pain is confirmed, multi-faceted (platform limitations, poor support, manual workarounds, low adoption), quantified where it matters (approval hours, invoice time, team capacity), and tied to a business imperative (headcount cap, $1.5B spend management). The missing element is executive-level acknowledgment that this pain justifies the cost and effort of a platform migration.

sources: Avoma 20 October 2025: 'Coupa contract expires in 2027; plan to issue an RFI to a small vendor shortlist, then proceed to RFP and transition by contract end' (no month specified)
Avoma 20 November 2025: current Workday ERP integration mentioned; no new-vendor contracting/legal process discussed
SF Opportunity fields: no Current_Contract_Expiration__c populated, no legal/InfoSec/procurement process fields
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, InfoSec, and implementation scoping were never discussed on any call and no Salesforce field captures them. The 20 October call confirmed Etsy uses Coupa CLM (previously Conga) and that Workday is the ERP for supplier master and financial record, so any new P2P vendor will require Workday integration for payments and supplier data sync. Jace mentioned on 20 November that 'Coupa CLM averages 14 days per contract' but this refers to current-state contract turnaround, not procurement for a new vendor. No MSA, SOW, security questionnaire, procurement channel (direct vs reseller/partner), or implementation timeline was explored. The Coupa contract expiry in 2027 (stated broadly on 20 October, no specific month confirmed) is the forcing function, but whether that is calendar year-end, fiscal year-end, or a mid-year renewal date is unclear. Etsy's fiscal year and procurement approval cycle timing are unknown. The paper process is a complete gap; the move is to ask Jace (if re-engaged) or her director what the procurement, legal, and InfoSec requirements are for a vendor transition of this scale, and to anchor the Coupa expiry date precisely so we can build a credible implementation and transition timeline.

economic_buyer.narrative: Budget authority sits above Jace Pennisi and was referenced but never directly engaged. Jace stated on 20 October 2025 that 'Director and VP-level stakeholders will assess product fit and cost savings before presenting to CEO, who approves based on trusted recommendations,' placing commercial sign-off at Director/VP level with CEO rubber-stamp for large spend. On 20 November she noted 'Strategic finance sign-off required for requisitions over ~$125K before budget approval,' confirming a finance gate. The Director she reports to is named 'Guru' (mentioned 20 November as the person she would present Zycus to post-Thanksgiving). No Director or VP was ever on a call, no CFO Lanny Baker engagement, no finance stakeholder mapped. Jace's plan was to secure internal budget approval post-Thanksgiving 2025 and then recommend Zycus for the RFI, but that step never happened (no follow-up logged after 5 January 2026 rep outreach). The Economic Buyer is unidentified by role and unengaged; we have spoken only to the procurement operations manager (Jace), who is an influencer/champion but does not control the commercial decision. The missing move is to map and engage Jace's director (Guru) and the VP-level stakeholder who will present to the CEO, plus the strategic finance approver.

SF LastActivityDate 9 February 2026 with no detail; no subsequent activity for 137 days
decision_process.narrative: The stated process on 20 October 2025 was: (1) exploratory vendor demos through December 2025, (2) draft and issue RFI to a small shortlist by January 2026, (3) RFI response evaluation, (4) shortlist to RFP, (5) Director/VP assessment of product fit and cost savings, (6) VP presentation to CEO for final approval, (7) transition off Coupa by contract expiry in 2027. Jace planned to include Zycus in the RFI after securing post-Thanksgiving 2025 budget approval to fund the procurement transformation initiative. The call sequence shows the process stalled at step 1: two exploration calls (15 and 20 October 2025) and one demo workshop (20 November 2025), then silence. No RFI was issued, no budget approval confirmed, no director or VP engaged, no stakeholder expansion beyond Jace. The rep's 5 January 2026 follow-up asked if Jace secured the go-ahead for 2026 plans but no reply is logged. The actual decision process Etsy is running is now unknown—they may have paused the procurement transformation, deprioritized it, or moved forward with other vendors without Zycus. The stated approval chain (Jace → Director Guru → VP → CEO, with strategic finance gate at $125K+) remains the map, but no progress along it is documented.

sources: Avoma 20 October 2025, Jace Pennisi: 'Slack integration for intake is a mandatory requirement and will influence whether Zycus progresses to the RFI stage'

Avoma 20 November 2025 demo notes: 'Zycus demo met key requirements with Slack integration, AI-driven intake, dynamic approval workflows, contract utilization alerts, and automated two-/three-way matching'
Avoma 20 November 2025, Jace Pennisi: 'This is already way easier than what my team has to do today in Coupa. Oh my god.'
Avoma 20 November 2025: 'Contract management needs included real-time expiry reminders, Word-based redlining, and utilization tracking, all of which Zycus supports organically on one data platform'
decision_criteria.narrative: Etsy's evaluation criteria were explicitly stated across both calls and validated during the demo workshop. Mandatory requirements: (1) Slack integration for conversational procurement intake—Jace said on 20 October 2025 'Slack integration for intake is a mandatory requirement and will influence whether Zycus progresses to the RFI stage'; (2) dynamic approval workflows that auto-bypass when PO and chart of accounts match, eliminating the 120 annual approval hours (20 October, 20 November); (3) intelligent invoice extraction and matching to eliminate dummy AP records and 5-minute manual review per invoice (20 November); (4) supplier self-service portal replacing the Certa manual copy workflow (20 November); (5) post-signature CLM with Word-based redlining, renewal/expiry alerts, utilization tracking, and obligation management (20 November). The 20 November demo validated all five: 'Zycus demo met key requirements with Slack integration, AI-driven intake, dynamic approval workflows, contract utilization alerts, and automated two-/three-way matching.' Jace's reaction was 'This is already way easier than what my team has to do today in Coupa. Oh my god.' Additional nice-to-haves included budget tracking at requisition time and branded supplier portal with discussion threads. The RFI was planned for January 2026 to formalize these into a scored evaluation, but it was never issued.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Etsy is AI Hungry. Jace stated on 20 October 2025 that poor user adoption of Coupa's rigid UI is a major pain and users 'prefer natural language of Slack.' She described a Slack AI bot pilot on 20 November 2025 that deflected 40% of procurement queries with <30 min response time, proving appetite for conversational AI. Slack integration for conversational intake was the single mandatory requirement that would 'influence whether Zycus progresses to the RFI stage.' The demo validated AI-driven invoice extraction, fraud detection, and Merlin intake, and Jace's reaction was enthusiastic ('way easier'). Etsy is a software-centric, nimble company that values innovation and is already experimenting with AI internally. However, no AIS score is logged in Salesforce, so this read is built entirely from call evidence. The AI fit is confirmed and strong; the gap is that we never leveraged it into a formal AI-led business case or executive engagement.

champion_strength.summary: Jace Pennisi was a strong early champion (October–November 2025): she articulated pain, defined requirements, validated the demo enthusiastically, and committed to recommending Zycus for the RFI post-Thanksgiving budget approval. However, she has been completely silent for 137 days since 9 February 2026 with no reply to the rep's 5 January follow-up. Her current status is at-risk. The relationship is single-threaded (no other buyer stakeholder engaged) and fragile. It is unknown whether she could not secure internal approval, the project was deprioritized, or she is evaluating other vendors. Until she re-engages and re-confirms intent, this deal has no active champion.

confidence_signals.summary: Deal is cold. Last buyer engagement 9 February 2026 (137 days ago). Champion Jace Pennisi was enthusiastic through 20 November 2025 demo, committed to post-Thanksgiving budget approval and RFI inclusion, then disappeared. Rep follow-up 5 January 2026 received no reply. No stakeholder expansion, no RFI received, no forward motion in nearly five months. Single-threaded on a manager with no budget authority. Unknown whether project paused, budget denied, or other vendors engaged. This is a ghost deal unless re-engagement succeeds.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 21 October 2025, qualified same cycle. LastActivityDate 9 February 2026 = 137 days ago. Stage = Qualified (earliest evaluation stage). Days to close = 273. Close date 26 March 2027 is a placeholder; no evidence of a buyer-driven timeline tied to Coupa contract expiry (stated as 2027 on 20 October 2025 call but not anchored to a specific month). Time in Qualified stage exceeds 8 months with no stage progression. No field-history stage movement, no new calls, no Tasks/Events since February. Rep outreach 5 January 2026 per Next_Step yielded no documented buyer reply. This deal requires full re-engagement before any commercial or technical step.
evidence: Last buyer call: 20 November 2025 demo workshop (~7 months ago). Jace Pennisi (champion) said post-Thanksgiving budget approval would trigger RFI inclusion decision; no follow-up logged.
Rep outreach 5 January 2026 per Next_Step: follow-up email to Jace asking if she secured go-ahead to include Procurement Transformation in 2026 plans. No logged reply, no subsequent activity.
LastActivityDate 9 February 2026 (137 days ago) with no call, email, or task detail explaining what occurred.
Zero forward motion since November 2025 demo: no RFI received, no stakeholder expansion, no commercial discussion, no December tailored session (planned on 20 October call) ever scheduled.
Current forecast Pipeline / $250k is unsupported by engagement, champion momentum, or buyer timeline confirmation.
Stage Qualified for 8+ months with no progression signals deal never advanced past early exploration despite strong early discovery fit.
north_star_verdict.headline: Deal dark for 137 days since 9 February 2026 buyer contact. Champion Jace never responded to January outreach; promised post-Thanksgiving RFI decision never materialized. No stage progression in 8+ months from Qualified. This is a dead thread requiring full re-qualification.

best_practice_check.summary: This deal requires a full restart, not incremental moves. The champion has been silent for 137 days and the buyer's internal process (RFI, budget approval, stakeholder alignment) is a black box. The three highest-leverage actions are: (1) multi-channel re-engagement to Jace with a new forcing event (Slack integration live, new customer proof point, Coupa expiry urgency) and a fallback escalation to her director if no reply within 10 business days; (2) ruthless disqualification decision by mid-July if re-engagement fails—this deal cannot sit dark in Pipeline forecast through 2027 consuming forecast credibility; (3) if Jace re-engages, immediately expand the thread to her Director (Guru) and strategic finance to build multi-level sponsorship so the deal is not single-threaded on a manager who has already ghosted once.

competitive_position.summary: Coupa is the sole documented competitor—the incumbent being displaced, contract expiring 2027. Etsy's pain with Coupa is severe and multi-dimensional (aging platform, poor support, inflexible workflows, weak AI, low user adoption). Zycus validated strong product fit on 20 November 2025 demo, especially on mandatory Slack integration and approval/AP automation. However, Jace's stated plan was to run an RFI to 3–5 vendors in January 2026, and the 137-day silence suggests either (a) the RFI went out and Zycus was excluded, (b) other vendors are being evaluated without us, or (c) the entire initiative was paused/killed. No other P2P vendor was named on calls, but standard RFI practice would bring in Ivalua, SAP Ariba, Jaggaer, GEP, or Zip as alternatives. Current competitive threat is unknown due to complete lack of visibility; the primary risk is that we are out of the process entirely and unaware.

competitors.name: Coupa (incumbent)
competitors.quote: Coupa feels aging and not built for a nimble company like Etsy… no face-to-face interaction… just give us community links… major issues impacting business - 50% supplier adoption, poor user adoption, very manual
competitors.how_we_win: Displace on validated pain: Slack-native conversational intake (mandatory requirement, Coupa lacks), dynamic approval bypass eliminating 120 annual hours (Coupa inflexible thresholds), intelligent invoice extraction cutting 5 min/invoice AP time (Coupa poor extraction), single-platform CLM with Word redlining and utilization tracking (Coupa bolted modules, 14-day cycle), and proactive partnership model (Coupa poor support). Anchor to Coupa contract expiry 2027 forcing function and quantify ROI (approval hours, AP capacity, user adoption improvement). Wedge validated 20 November 2025; activation requires re-engagement, Director/finance sponsorship, and proof Slack integration is live.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning was strong and well-received in the November 2025 demo. Jace enthusiastically validated the Merlin conversational intake in Slack ('mandatory requirement'), the AI-driven invoice extraction and fraud detection ('way easier than what my team has to do today'), and the Slack AI bot she is already piloting internally (40% query deflection, <30 min response time). Etsy is AI-forward and specifically seeking natural-language, conversational procurement to drive user adoption (stated pain: 'poor user adoption, prefer natural language of Slack' per 5 January Next_Step). The AI narrative was the wedge that differentiated Zycus from Coupa. However, 137 days of silence means we have no current visibility into whether AI remains a priority or whether Etsy's AI strategy has shifted. The positioning was right; the execution (staying engaged, proving Slack integration shipped, anchoring AI ROI to the 120 approval hours and 5 min/invoice pain) never happened.

items.quote: Slack integration for intake is a mandatory requirement and will influence whether Zycus progresses to the RFI stage. Zycus Slack integration expected by end of this year.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 20 October 2025, Jace Pennisi, and Zycus roadmap confirmation
items.criterion: Slack integration for conversational procurement intake

items.quote: Cut redundant manual approval hours by bypassing approval if PO and chart of accounts match. 120 hours/year saved in approval workflows by automating matched requisitions.
items.criterion: Dynamic approval workflows that auto-bypass when PO and chart of accounts match

items.quote: Improve invoice extraction and matching to eliminate dummy records and reduce manual intervention. AP Smartdesk for email invoice intake: fraud/duplicate detection, OCR extraction, auto-draft, policy checks.
items.criterion: Intelligent invoice extraction and matching to eliminate dummy AP records

items.quote: Supplier portal with branded self-registration, PO/invoice status, dashboards, and discussions. Supplier onboarding and portal in Coupa is cumbersome, whereas Zycus offers branded self-service registration and unified PO/invoice access at no cost to suppliers.
items.criterion: Supplier self-service portal replacing Certa manual copy workflow

items.quote: Contract management needs included real-time expiry reminders, Word-based redlining, and utilization tracking, all of which Zycus supports organically on one data platform. CLM AI engines: contract discovery metadata, legal-risk review, clause library, in-Word redlining.
items.criterion: Post-signature CLM with Word-based redlining, renewal/expiry alerts, utilization tracking

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Etsy's stated requirements were validated point-by-point in the 20 November 2025 demo workshop. Mandatory Slack integration for conversational intake was confirmed on Zycus roadmap for end of 2025 (needs re-confirmation that it shipped). Dynamic approval workflows, intelligent invoice extraction, supplier self-service portal, and post-signature CLM with Word redlining and utilization tracking were all demonstrated and met enthusiastic approval from Jace ('This is already way easier than what my team has to do today in Coupa'). Product fit is strong; the gap is not technical but engagement—no buyer contact for 137 days means we cannot confirm whether the business case, budget, or priority alignment still holds.

hard.ais_why:
• Jace Pennisi called Slack-integrated conversational procurement intake a hard requirement and the gating criterion for whether Zycus is invited to the RFI.
• She also flagged Coupa CLM as having no AI today and invited Zycus to position its CLM as an alternative, opening an explicit AI-displacement window.
• Two recorded calls including a 104-minute Nov demo workshop plus an active RFI/RFP roadmap to 2027 give high-confidence Hungry read.
hard.next_step: 1/05/26  RH - F/up email to Jace - did she secure go-ahead to include Procurement Transformation in 26' plans. Major issues with Coupa impacting business - 50% supplier adoption, poor user adoption (prefer natural language of Slack) very manual, tedious intervention on invoices.
